title: QU exception and literal mode mismatch

content.text:
Deeply verify the spec @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and tests, production code at @src/lex.zig 

- Find out the mismatch and report to me concisely.
- What could we improve in the tests to gain more critical coverage. I don't want full coverage but I want important points get covered.

input.command: pwd; find . -maxdepth 3 -type f | sort | sed 's#^./##' | grep -E '(^specs/|^src/|test|build\.zig|AGENTS\.md)'

result.output:
1	# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
     2	
     3	The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
     4	
     5	## Vietnamese Telex Rules
     6	
     7	### Literal input characters
     8	
     9	- When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
    10	  input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
    11	  don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
    12	- When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
    13	  character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
    14	  Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
    15	  lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
    16	- Literal input remains until the new word.
    17	- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
    18	
    19	### Diacritics
    20	
    21	Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
    22	Tones have a separate section.
    23	
    24	#### Empty
    25	
    26	- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
    27	- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
    28	  characters.
    29	
    30	#### Circumflex
    31	
    32	- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
    33	
    34	#### Breve
    35	
    36	- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
    37	
    38	#### Horn
    39	
    40	- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
    41	
    42	#### Stroke
    43	
    44	- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
    45	
    46	#### Apply rules
    47	
    48	- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
    49	- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
    50	  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
    51	  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
    52	  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
    53	- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
    54	  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
    55	  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
    56	  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
    57	- The existing character keep its case, tone.
    58	- Treat out of scope characters as literal.
    59	
    60	#### Override rules
    61	
    62	- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
    63	  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
    64	  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
    65	- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
    66	- Keep tone untouched.
    67	- The existing character keep its case, tone.
    68	- Treat out of scope characters as literal.
    69	
    70	#### Cancel rules
    71	
    72	- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
    73	  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
    74	- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
    75	  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
    76	  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
    77	  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
    78	  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
    79	- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
    80	- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
    81	  remains until the new word.
    82	  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
    83	- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
    84	
    85	#### Auto-fill rules
    86	
    87	- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
    88	  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
    89	- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
    90	  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
    91	  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
    92	  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
    93	- The new input character is appended literally.
    94	- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
    95	
    96	### Tones
    97	
    98	Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
    99	Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
   100	These vowel could take any tone.
   101	
   102	#### Level (ngang)
   103	
   104	- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
   105	  corresponding variants.
   106	- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
   107	
   108	#### Rising (sắc)
   109	
   110	- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
   111	- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
   112	  -> `Ớ`.
   113	
   114	#### Falling (huyền)
   115	
   116	- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
   117	- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
   118	  -> `Ờ`.
   119	
   120	#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
   121	
   122	- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
   123	- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
   124	  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
   125	
   126	#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
   127	
   128	- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
   129	- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
   130	  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
   131	
   132	#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
   133	
   134	- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
   135	- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
   136	  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
   137	
   138	#### Apply rules
   139	
   140	- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
   141	  character literally.
   142	- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
   143	  their lower cases.
   144	- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
   145	- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
   146	- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
   147	  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
   148	- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
   149	
   150	#### Cancel rules
   151	
   152	- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
   153	  character literally.
   154	- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
   155	  their lower cases.
   156	- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
   157	- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
   158	- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
   159	  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
   160	- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
   161	  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
   162	- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
   163	
   164	#### Reset rules
   165	
   166	- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
   167	  character literally.
   168	- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
   169	- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
   170	- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
   171	  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
   172	- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
   173	  Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
   174	- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
   175	  new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
   176	  characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
   177	- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
   178	
   179	#### Override rules
   180	
   181	- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
   182	  character literally.
   183	- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
   184	  their lower cases.
   185	- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
   186	- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
   187	- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
   188	  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
   189	  the word. The override tone is always successful.
   190	
   191	#### Scan pseudo-word
   192	
   193	- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
   194	  the tone in the correct position.
   195	- The existing characters must not be empty.
   196	- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
   197	- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
   198	  character to scan, then stop.
   199	- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
   200	  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
   201	- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
   202	  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
   203	  `<consonant><vowels>`.
   204	
   205	#### Tone position rules
   206	
   207	The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
   208	
   209	- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
   210	  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
   211	- Exceptions:
   212	  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
   213	- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
   214	- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
   215	  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
   216	  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
   217	    listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
   218	  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
   219	    `U` count as part of the consonant.
   220	  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
   221	  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
   222	    consonants, put on second vowel.
   223	  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
   224	- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
   225	- Could not put tone for other cases.

input.task: Review the Vietnamese Telex spec, tests embedded in src/lex.zig, and production code for mismatches. Focus on behavioral mismatches and high-value missing test coverage, not style. Return concise findings with line-level evidence where possible.
input.context: The user wants a concise report: mismatches among spec/tests/production code, and what tests should improve to cover important points. Do not propose broad full coverage; identify critical gaps.

run.result:
## TL;DR

Spec/tests/code are mostly aligned, but there is one important behavioral inconsistency around `QU` vs `QƯ`, plus several high-value missing tests around `Z`, literal-tail recovery, and tone-position priority. I would fix/clarify the `QU` rule first, then add a small number of targeted tests rather than expanding broad coverage.

## Behavioral mismatches / inconsistencies

1) **`QU` exception is implemented inconsistently for diacritized `U` (`QƯ`)**

- Spec says only word exactly `QU` cannot receive tone: `specs/...md:211-213`.
- Some tone branches check exact plain `QU`:
  - `F`: requires `U` with `.empty` + `.level` at `src/lex.zig:821`
  - `R/S/X`: same plain `U` check at `1119`, `1183`, `1389`
- `J` checks broader `Q` + `U` ignoring diacritic/tone at `src/lex.zig:926`.
- `apply_tone` also asserts broader `Q` + any `U`-base, ignoring diacritic/tone at `src/lex.zig:1610-1611`.

Impact: input like `quw` produces `qư`; then:
- `quwj` is treated as literal `j` because `J` uses broad `Q`+`U`.
- `quws/quwf/quwr/quwx` can pass branch checks but hit the broader `apply_tone` assertion in debug/safe builds.
- Under the written spec, `qư` is not exactly `QU`, so it should either accept tone placement or the spec should explicitly say all `Q` + `U`-base variants are consonant-only.

**Recommended test/fix target:** decide intended behavior, then add one focused test set for `quw{s,f,r,x,j}`. If following current spec literally, expected outputs are `qứ/qừ/qử/qữ/qự`.

2) **Non-alphabet / word-boundary behavior is unclear between spec and production**

- Spec repeatedly says literal input remains until “new word” and backspace can pass before the literal point: `specs/...md:16-17`, `80-83`, `160-162`, `175-177`.
- `State.add`/`lex_add` only accept ASCII alphabet and assert otherwise: `src/lex.zig:630-632`.
- There is no explicit “new word” API in `src/lex.zig`; callers must presumably reset/re-init state or handle separators externally.

This may be fine if the embedding layer owns spaces/punctuation/new-word reset, but the spec should say that. If `lex_add` is intended to receive separators, production currently does not match “treat as literal/new word”.

## High-value missing test coverage

1) **`Z` reset rules are under-tested**

Spec has three important `Z` branches: reset existing tone, append literal on no vowels but remain Vietnamese, append literal and switch literal on level vowels: `specs/...md:164-177`.

Current tests cover:
- start-of-buffer literal `Z`: `src/lex.zig:2666-2687`
- no-vowel append state: `2824-2900`
- simple compose cases `dz`, `ngZ`: `3834-3841`

Missing critical cases:
- `asz` / seeded `á + z` => reset to `a`, no `z` appended, no literal tail. Code path: `1472-1475`.
- `az` => append `z` and set `literal_index` because vowel has level tone. Code path: `1477-1480`.
- End-to-end spec example `dzux` => `dzũ`, proving `Z` after no-vowel pseudo-word does **not** start literal mode. Spec example: `specs/...md:172-173`.

2) **Literal-tail backspace recovery is only tested at buffer-boundary, not normal literal points**

Spec emphasizes backspacing before literal point restores Vietnamese mode: `specs/...md:17`, `83`, `162`, `177`.

Current backspace tests focus on full-buffer literal index `15`: `src/lex.zig:3435-3471`.

Add one normal-case test:
- `aaa` gives cancel result `aa` with `literal_index = 1`; backspace once removes the literal `a` and clears `literal_index`; adding `a` again should produce `â`.

This directly protects the common literal-tail path from diacritic cancellation, not just the buffer-limit path.

3) **Tone-cancel literal persistence is not tested after the cancellation step**

Tone cancellation itself is tested at `src/lex.zig:3171-3297`, but not that subsequent tone triggers stay literal.

Add one compact test:
- `ass` cancels `á` to `a` + literal `s` with `literal_index = 1`.
- Next `s` should append literally, not retone the earlier `a`.
- Expected effective text: `ass`, not `ás`.

This covers the spec’s “literal input remains” rule for tone cancellation: `specs/...md:158-162`.

4) **Tone-position priority conflict: `QU` vs exact `UY`**

Tests cover exact `UY` and `QU + A` separately in `apply_tone`: `src/lex.zig:2927-2934`, `2945-2949`, but not the priority conflict.

Spec priority puts `GI/QU` before exact `UY`: `specs/...md:218-220`.

Add one test:
- `quys` should tone `Y`: `quý`, not `qúy`.

This is small but high-value because `quy/quỳnh/quý` are common and exercise rule ordering.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
173:   Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
175:   new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
176:   characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
177: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
178: 
179: #### Override rules
180: 
181: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
182:   character literally.
183: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
184:   their lower cases.
185: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
186: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
187: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
188:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
189:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
190: 
191: #### Scan pseudo-word
192: 
193: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
194:   the tone in the correct position.
195: - The existing characters must not be empty.
196: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
197: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
198:   character to scan, then stop.
199: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
200:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
201: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
202:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
203:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
204: 
205: #### Tone position rules
206: 
207: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
208: 
209: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
210:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
211: - Exceptions:
212:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
213: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
214: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
215:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
216:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
217:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
218:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
219:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
220:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
221:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
222:     consonants, put on second vowel.
223:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
224: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
225: - Could not put tone for other cases.

input.question: What context, recent changes, and specific concerns led to the request to review the Vietnamese Telex spec, tests in src/lex.zig, and production code for behavioral mismatches and missing test coverage?

tool_uses.result:
The request to review the Vietnamese Telex specification, the tests in `src/lex.zig`, and the production code for behavioral mismatches and missing test coverage was driven by the following context, recent changes, and concerns:

* **Context & Recent Changes:** The project implements a Vietnamese Telex input method engine in Zig. Recent development (as shown in the agent-threads log) involved a series of refactorings and feature additions, including rewriting circumflex and tone tests with parameterization, designing test cases for diacritic literal appending, updating tests to reduce compile times, writing pseudoword scanning tests, and implementing synthetic backspaces and UTF-16 composition/replacement tests.
* **Specific Concerns:** The primary concern was to deeply verify that the specification (`specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md`) matches the production code and tests in `src/lex.zig`. Specifically, the goal was to identify any behavioral mismatches between the documented Telex rules (such as literal input triggers, diacritic overrides/cancellations, auto-fill rules, tone position rules, and pseudoword scanning) and their actual implementation, while identifying critical, high-value gaps in test coverage rather than aiming for broad, low-value full coverage.

progress.message:
## TL;DR

Spec/tests/code are mostly aligned, but there is one important behavioral inconsistency around `QU` vs `QƯ`, plus several high-value missing tests around `Z`, literal-tail recovery, and tone-position priority. I would fix/clarify the `QU` rule first, then add a small number of targeted tests rather than expanding broad coverage.

## Behavioral mismatches / inconsistencies

1) **`QU` exception is implemented inconsistently for diacritized `U` (`QƯ`)**

- Spec says only word exactly `QU` cannot receive tone: `specs/...md:211-213`.
- Some tone branches check exact plain `QU`:
  - `F`: requires `U` with `.empty` + `.level` at `src/lex.zig:821`
  - `R/S/X`: same plain `U` check at `1119`, `1183`, `1389`
- `J` checks broader `Q` + `U` ignoring diacritic/tone at `src/lex.zig:926`.
- `apply_tone` also asserts broader `Q` + any `U`-base, ignoring diacritic/tone at `src/lex.zig:1610-1611`.

Impact: input like `quw` produces `qư`; then:
- `quwj` is treated as literal `j` because `J` uses broad `Q`+`U`.
- `quws/quwf/quwr/quwx` can pass branch checks but hit the broader `apply_tone` assertion in debug/safe builds.
- Under the written spec, `qư` is not exactly `QU`, so it should either accept tone placement or the spec should explicitly say all `Q` + `U`-base variants are consonant-only.

**Recommended test/fix target:** decide intended behavior, then add one focused test set for `quw{s,f,r,x,j}`. If following current spec literally, expected outputs are `qứ/qừ/qử/qữ/qự`.

2) **Non-alphabet / word-boundary behavior is unclear between spec and production**

- Spec repeatedly says literal input remains until “new word” and backspace can pass before the literal point: `specs/...md:16-17`, `80-83`, `160-162`, `175-177`.
- `State.add`/`lex_add` only accept ASCII alphabet and assert otherwise: `src/lex.zig:630-632`.
- There is no explicit “new word” API in `src/lex.zig`; callers must presumably reset/re-init state or handle separators externally.

This may be fine if the embedding layer owns spaces/punctuation/new-word reset, but the spec should say that. If `lex_add` is intended to receive separators, production currently does not match “treat as literal/new word”.

## High-value missing test coverage

1) **`Z` reset rules are under-tested**

Spec has three important `Z` branches: reset existing tone, append literal on no vowels but remain Vietnamese, append literal and switch literal on level vowels: `specs/...md:164-177`.

Current tests cover:
- start-of-buffer literal `Z`: `src/lex.zig:2666-2687`
- no-vowel append state: `2824-2900`
- simple compose cases `dz`, `ngZ`: `3834-3841`

Missing critical cases:
- `asz` / seeded `á + z` => reset to `a`, no `z` appended, no literal tail. Code path: `1472-1475`.
- `az` => append `z` and set `literal_index` because vowel has level tone. Code path: `1477-1480`.
- End-to-end spec example `dzux` => `dzũ`, proving `Z` after no-vowel pseudo-word does **not** start literal mode. Spec example: `specs/...md:172-173`.

2) **Literal-tail backspace recovery is only tested at buffer-boundary, not normal literal points**

Spec emphasizes backspacing before literal point restores Vietnamese mode: `specs/...md:17`, `83`, `162`, `177`.

Current backspace tests focus on full-buffer literal index `15`: `src/lex.zig:3435-3471`.

Add one normal-case test:
- `aaa` gives cancel result `aa` with `literal_index = 1`; backspace once removes the literal `a` and clears `literal_index`; adding `a` again should produce `â`.

This directly protects the common literal-tail path from diacritic cancellation, not just the buffer-limit path.

3) **Tone-cancel literal persistence is not tested after the cancellation step**

Tone cancellation itself is tested at `src/lex.zig:3171-3297`, but not that subsequent tone triggers stay literal.

Add one compact test:
- `ass` cancels `á` to `a` + literal `s` with `literal_index = 1`.
- Next `s` should append literally, not retone the earlier `a`.
- Expected effective text: `ass`, not `ás`.

This covers the spec’s “literal input remains” rule for tone cancellation: `specs/...md:158-162`.

4) **Tone-position priority conflict: `QU` vs exact `UY`**

Tests cover exact `UY` and `QU + A` separately in `apply_tone`: `src/lex.zig:2927-2934`, `2945-2949`, but not the priority conflict.

Spec priority puts `GI/QU` before exact `UY`: `specs/...md:218-220`.

Add one test:
- `quys` should tone `Y`: `quý`, not `qúy`.

This is small but high-value because `quy/quỳnh/quý` are common and exercise rule ordering.

content.thinking:
**Examining code consistency**

I need to find exact line numbers for the spec, code, and tests, and include file links. My final response should be concise, but I might need more confidence in identifying mismatches. For instance, there could be a production issue with the test named "State.add appends Z literally when pseudoword has no vowel." I’m resetting the rules for when a pseudoword has no vowels and ensuring that I properly apply the appending logic based on the inputs.

**Analyzing buffer and tone application**

For the U branch, when the buffer length is 2 and doesn't match the uo pattern, it appends "u." The pseudoword starts from the vowel "u," and working backward, I find a consonant "z" which makes the word "zu" with length 2. I apply X to "u" to get "dzũ." It seems the immediate no-vowel reset for Z is tested, not in full examples. There’s a potential mismatch with the `apply_tone` function regarding the exact QU check, raising some concerns about tone handling.

**Exploring tone application and mismatches**

I’m considering that the code should never produce "qú" because the exact QU can’t have a tone, but if the state is seeded in tests, that might change. It's not user input, but for "QƯ," it’s more likely derived from "quw." The F branch needs U to be empty at the level, while QƯ goes to `apply_tone` for an assertion. The J branch appends "j," treating it as exact. R, S, and X branches have the same assertion, which indicates a real mismatch that I should definitely include.

**Verifying tests for production mismatch**

I need to verify that tests match up with production. For the test "State.apply_tone places tone at correct vowel," it checks if `UY` precedes `GI` or `QU`. However, I realize that the order in the code introduces a special consonant before the exact `UY`. The test expects exact `UY` seeds of {u,y}, yet it looks like that should be zero based on this logic. I've got to clarify this!

**Analyzing code mismatch**

The user is asking about the production code in `src/lex.zig`. I see that the spec mentions "new word," but `src/lex.zig` lacks a word-boundary function. The caller might use `lex_init`, but we need to be cautious here; if the input method host handles separators, it might not be a real mismatch. I think it’s important to report this as a “spec/code contract missing.” I’ll keep it concise and mention the ambiguity without labeling it as a bug. Running grep for "Z" tests could be beneficial, but it seems unnecessary.

content.text:
## Result

I found one material production/spec mismatch and one spec/API ambiguity. Current tests pass: `zig test src/lex.zig` → 43/43.

## Mismatch

1. **`QU` exception is inconsistent for `QƯ`**
   - Spec excludes only word exactly `QU` from tone placement: [spec](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L211-L213).
   - Production sometimes checks plain `U`, e.g. `F/R/S/X`: [F check](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L820-L830), but `J` treats any `U` base as `QU`, ignoring diacritic/tone: [J check](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L925-L935).
   - `apply_tone` also asserts `Q` + any `U` base, ignoring diacritic/tone: [assert](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L1609-L1612).
   - Consequence: after `quw` → `qư`, `quwj` becomes literal `qưj`, while `quws/quwf/quwr/quwx` can hit the `apply_tone` assert in safe/debug builds. Under the written spec, `QƯ` is not exactly `QU`, so it should probably accept tones: `qứ/qừ/qử/qữ/qự`, unless the spec intends `Q` + all `U` variants to be consonant-only.

2. **“New word” boundary is underspecified for this engine**
   - Spec says literal mode remains until a new word/backspace before literal point: [literal rule](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L16-L17).
   - `State.add` / `lex_add` only accept alphabetic bytes and assert otherwise: [code](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L630-L632).
   - This may be fine if the caller resets state on spaces/punctuation, but the spec should state that contract.

## Critical test improvements

1. **Add `QƯ` regression tests**
   - Decide intended behavior first.
   - If following current spec literally, cover `quws`, `quwf`, `quwr`, `quwx`, `quwj` and expect tone on `ư`.
   - This directly catches the production mismatch above.

2. **Cover all important `Z` reset branches**
   Existing tests cover `Z` on empty/no-vowel cases, but not the main reset behavior. Add:
   - `asz` → `a`, no appended `z`, no literal tail.
   - `az` → `az`, `literal_index` starts at `z`.
   - `dzux` → `dzũ`, proving `z` after no-vowel pseudo-word remains Vietnamese-processable, matching the spec example: [spec](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L172-L173).

3. **Test literal-tail persistence after tone cancellation**
   - Existing tone-cancel test checks the immediate transition: [test](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L3171-L3297).
   - Add continuation, e.g. `asss` should stay `ass`, not re-tone earlier `a`.

4. **Test backspace recovery from a normal literal point**
   - Current backspace tests mostly cover buffer-boundary literal mode: [tests](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig#L3435-L3471).
   - Add: `aaa` creates `aa` with literal tail; backspace clears literal mode; adding `a` again should produce `â`.

5. **Add one tone-position priority conflict**
   - `quys` should tone `y` → `quý`, because `QU` special-start priority comes before exact `UY`: [spec](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L218-L220).
   - This is small and high-value because it protects rule ordering, not just individual rules.
